flashcards.front: What is thrashing in virtual memory systems?
flashcards.back: Thrashing is a severe performance degradation state where the CPU spends most of its time swapping pages in and out of memory instead of executing processes, due to excessive paging activity caused by insufficient physical memory.

Thrashing occurs when there are too many processes competing for too few memory frames, leading to a high page fault rate. The system spends more time servicing page faults than executing instructions.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and thrashing?
flashcards.back: As the degree of multiprogramming increases (more processes in memory), CPU utilization initially increases but eventually drops sharply when thrashing begins due to excessive page swapping.

flashcards.front: What is the Working Set Model?
flashcards.back: The Working Set Model defines the set of pages a process has referenced in the last τ (tau) time units. The system must allocate enough frames to accommodate each process's working set to prevent thrashing.

flashcards.front: What is Page Fault Frequency (PFF) control?
flashcards.back: PFF is a thrashing control strategy that directly monitors the page fault rate of each process. If the fault rate exceeds an upper threshold, frames are allocated; if below a lower threshold, frames are reclaimed.

flashcards.front: What are the symptoms of thrashing?
flashcards.back: Symptoms include: extremely high page fault rate, CPU utilization dropping below 20-30%, disk I/O activity becoming very high, processes spending most time in ready state waiting for page I/O.

flashcards.front: What is local versus global page replacement in context of thrashing?
flashcards.back: Local replacement restricts each process to its own allocated frames, maintaining that process's working set. Global replacement allows any process to take frames from any other, which can accelerate thrashing.

flashcards.front: How does thrashing affect CPU utilization?
flashcards.back: During thrashing, CPU utilization drops dramatically because the CPU is idle waiting for page I/O operations to complete. This creates a 'thrash curve' where utilization peaks then falls sharply.

flashcards.front: What is the thrashing curve or working set diagram?
flashcards.back: The thrashing curve plots CPU utilization versus degree of multiprogramming. It shows a peak at optimal multiprogramming level, then a sharp decline into thrashing zone as more processes compete for limited memory.

flashcards.front: How can thrashing be prevented or reduced?
flashcards.back: Prevention methods include: adjusting degree of multiprogramming, using page fault frequency control, implementing working set model, priority-based allocation, and pre-paging strategies.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between thrashing and normal paging?
flashcards.back: Normal paging involves occasional page faults that are quickly serviced with minimal performance impact. Thrashing involves constant, repeated page faults causing the system to spend more time on paging than execution.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the idle process in thrashing detection?
flashcards.back: The idle process (or swapper) runs when the CPU has no ready processes. If the idle process is heavily active (swapping pages), it indicates the system is thrashing because processes are not getting enough frames.

flashcards.front: What is a valid reference string in paging?
flashcards.back: A reference string is a sequence of memory references (page numbers) used to analyze page replacement algorithms and simulate thrashing behavior under different memory allocation scenarios.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between frame allocation and thrashing?
flashcards.back: If each process does not receive enough frames to hold its working set, the process will generate page faults frequently. When many processes behave this way, system-wide thrashing occurs.

flashcards.front: What is priority replacement in thrashing control?
flashcards.back: Priority replacement gives preferential treatment to higher-priority processes when allocating frames, allowing lower-priority processes to lose frames. This helps critical processes avoid thrashing.
